turns.content: I just got my first working tests up with the Json wrapper using serde_json::Value for our config parsing, but I'm struggling to clean up temporary files in test fixtures without writing a bunch of manual Drop implementations everywhere. It feels like every test helper needs its own cleanup logic and that's getting messy.

That sounds frustrating when you are just starting out with test infrastructure. The pattern you described is exactly what the Test Helpers and Fixtures category covers, specifically using helper structs with Drop cleanup to ensure files are removed automatically. Instead of writing manual drop implementations for every single test, you can define a reusable struct that implements Drop so it handles file removal when the scope ends. This approach lets you use parametrized tests where a single factory generates multiple scenarios without duplicating setup code.
So I should avoid creating new structs for each test and instead reuse one generic helper? That sounds cleaner than what I have right now with manual cleanup.

Exactly, that is the core benefit. By defining one struct that implements Drop to handle file removal, you can attach it as a fixture in your tests via the rstest crate. This means you don't need separate structs for every test case; instead, you create a single factory or helper that manages resources and cleans them up automatically when the test runs.
I see now how to centralize this logic so my tests stay DRY without duplicating drop implementations.

That is the right intuition. Now your test suite will be much more maintainable because you only manage cleanup in one place, and you can easily extend it with parametrized tests or additional factories as your project grows.
